cards.front: What attack does DHCP snooping prevent?
cards.back: Rogue DHCP servers (DHCP spoofing) — an attacker hands out IPs with their own machine as the default gateway, becoming MITM. Also stops some DoS attacks like DHCP starvation (exhausting the legitimate server's pool by spamming Discovers).

cards.front: What's the difference between TRUSTED and UNTRUSTED ports in DHCP snooping?
cards.back: Trusted: allow DHCP server messages (Offer, Ack). Configure on uplinks toward the legitimate DHCP server. Untrusted: drop DHCP server messages — only client messages (Discover, Request) allowed. Default is untrusted (after enabling). Configure trusted with `ip dhcp snooping trust`.

cards.front: Enable DHCP snooping for VLAN 10 with Gi0/24 trusted toward the server.
cards.back:
```
ip dhcp snooping
ip dhcp snooping vlan 10
interface gi0/24
 ip dhcp snooping trust
```
Default rate-limit on untrusted ports protects against DHCP starvation: `ip dhcp snooping limit rate 10` (per second).

cards.front: What is the DHCP snooping binding table, and how is it built?
cards.back: Maps client MAC ↔ leased IP ↔ VLAN ↔ port. Built automatically as DHCP exchanges complete. Used by other features: DAI, IP Source Guard, and to detect lease theft. View with `show ip dhcp snooping binding`.

cards.front: What attack does Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) prevent?
cards.back: ARP spoofing/poisoning — attacker forges ARP replies so victims think attacker is the gateway (or some target). Becomes MITM. DAI inspects ARP packets and validates them against the DHCP snooping binding table; mismatched ARP packets are dropped.

cards.front: Enable DAI for VLAN 10, trusting Gi0/24 (uplink to other switches).
cards.back:
```
ip arp inspection vlan 10
interface gi0/24
 ip arp inspection trust
```
DAI requires the DHCP snooping binding table to be populated (or use ARP ACLs for static-IP hosts). Untrusted ports rate-limit ARP packets to prevent flood attacks.

cards.front: What is IP Source Guard, and what does it require?
cards.back: Per-port filter that drops IP packets whose source IP+MAC don't match the DHCP snooping binding (or static binding). Stops IP spoofing by clients. Requires DHCP snooping enabled. Configure: `ip verify source` on the access port.

cards.front: Which DHCP message types come from the SERVER vs the CLIENT?
cards.back: Server: OFFER, ACK, NAK. Client: DISCOVER, REQUEST, RELEASE, DECLINE, INFORM. DHCP snooping uses this to drop server messages received on untrusted ports (rogue DHCP server protection).

cards.front: What is DHCP Option 82, and how does DHCP snooping interact with it?
cards.back: Option 82 = DHCP Relay Agent Information Option — added by a relay agent (or DHCP-snooping-enabled switch) to identify the originating switchport. Cisco switches with DHCP snooping enabled, by default, ADD Option 82 to forwarded DHCP messages and DROP messages with Option 82 received on untrusted ports. Disable adding it with `no ip dhcp snooping information option`.

cards.front: What does DAI rate-limiting default to, and what triggers err-disable?
cards.back: By default, untrusted ports are rate-limited to 15 ARP packets per second. If exceeded, the port is err-disabled. Trusted ports have no rate-limit by default. Configure: `ip arp inspection limit rate <pps> [burst interval <s>]`. Recover with `errdisable recovery cause arp-inspection`.

cards.front: DAI: what fields does it inspect, and what does it check them against?
cards.back: Inspects the SENDER MAC and SENDER IP fields of ARP messages. Checks them against the DHCP snooping binding table AND any configured ARP ACLs (`arp access-list NAME` → `permit ip host <ip> mac host <mac>`, applied with `ip arp inspection filter NAME vlan N`). Optional extra checks: `ip arp inspection validate {src-mac | dst-mac | ip}`. Only inspects packets on UNTRUSTED ports.
